artifacts.content:
From: Michael Harris <maharris62@gmail.com>
To: Nurse Tasha Reynolds <tasha.reynolds@okrheumclinic.com>
Date: 2025-02-18 07:42
Subject: Pre-infusion paperwork corrections and scheduling question

Hi Tasha,

Thanks again for sending over the pre-infusion packet and the summary from my visit with Dr. Bennett last week. I went through the forms last night and just wanted to clear up a couple of details so everything matches what you have in the chart.

On the “Patient Information” page, it lists my weight as 228, but when I was in the office on 2/12 the nurse weighed me at 238.0 pounds with shoes off. I’ve been hovering around that number for the last few months, so please update it to 238.0 so the dosing and BMI are accurate.

Also, in the medication notes you mentioned “no issues with driving to early a.m. infusions.” I should probably explain: because of my DUI arrest on June 3, 2024 in Broken Arrow, I still have an ignition interlock device on my F-150 through at least December 2025. My probation officer, Mark Ellison at Tulsa County Court Services, warned me that some alcohol-based mouthwashes and even certain liquid meds can trigger a false positive first thing in the morning.

That’s why I asked about slightly later starts on infusion days at the Allergy, Asthma & Immunology Center on S Yale. If we can keep those around 9:30–10:00 a.m., it gives me time to do my pain meds, mouthwash, and the required 15–20 minute wait before I blow into the device and head over.

Let me know if you need me to sign anything else to note this.

Best,
Michael Harris
DOB: 11/14/1963
Cell: 918-555-0142

artifacts.content:
From: Karen Harris <karen.harris.ok@gmail.com>
To: Michael Harris <mike.harris62@gmail.com>
Date: 2024-11-20 09:02 PM
Subject: That scale is WRONG

Mike,

I know you’re stewing about what the nurse said today, so I wanted to put this in writing: that scale in Dr. Patel’s office has to be off.

You told me they wrote down 245 pounds, which is crazy because when you stepped on our bathroom scale Sunday morning (before breakfast, like you always insist), it said 233.4. That’s almost a 12-pound difference in four days, and you’ve actually been eating lighter with all this talk about gout and your joints.

Remember last year when the urgent care scale said something similar and then the tech laughed and said, “Yeah, this one runs heavy”? I really think it’s the same deal. I don’t want you beating yourself up or thinking you’ve suddenly ballooned. You’ve been trying—less beer, more salads, even if you grumble through them.

If it makes you feel better, we can stop by CVS this weekend and use their pharmacy scale just to get a third opinion. But please don’t let one weird reading make you feel worse about everything else you’re juggling. The doctor is more concerned with your uric acid and how you’re actually feeling day to day, not one number on a machine.

Love you,
Karen

P.S. I hid the leftover Halloween candy so you’ll stop "checking" the bowl every night. :)

metadata.subject: That scale is WRONG

artifacts.content:
Title: Nutrition Consult – Weight & Gout Management
Date: 2024-10-15 08:30 AM–09:15 AM
Location: Saint Francis Hospital – Outpatient Nutrition, 2nd Floor
Attendees: Michael Harris; Rebecca Collins, RD/LD

Notes:

Initial appointment with dietitian at Dr. Bennett’s recommendation.

Talking points Michael jotted down when booking:
- Ask if current weight around 236 lbs is making gout worse or if it’s mostly genetics/meds.
- Clarify realistic goal: doctor mentioned maybe losing 15–20 lbs over the next year to take stress off ankles and big toes.
- Questions about cutting back on barbecue, red meat, and beer without feeling like there’s nothing left to enjoy.
- How much does weight really affect uric acid levels vs. just the purine content in food?

Background:
Michael has struggled with gradual weight gain over the last decade, especially since hitting his late 50s. He’s not thrilled about the number on the scale but is more worried about how heavy he feels climbing ladders at work and getting up off the couch when his joints are flaring. His rheumatologist suggested that even a modest drop in weight could help his feet and ankles, plus improve sleep and blood pressure.

Action items after visit (to add):
- Weekly weigh-ins at home (Sunday mornings) and log in notebook.
- Discuss with Karen about swapping out some takeout nights for simple home-cooked meals.
- Ask Rebecca about safe, low-impact exercises that won’t aggravate his gout but still support weight loss.

metadata.title: Nutrition Consult – Weight & Gout Management
metadata.location: Saint Francis Hospital – Outpatient Nutrition, 2nd Floor

Date: 2024-09-09 03:41 PM
Subject: Annual Wellness Screening Results (Confidential)

Hi Michael,

Thank you for participating in the company’s annual biometric screening on 09/05/2024. Below is a summary of the measurements provided by the on-site nurse practitioner.

Biometric results:
- Height: 70 in
- Weight: 242 lbs (boots removed, work pants and t‑shirt)
- Waist circumference: 43 in
- Blood pressure: 138/82
- Fasting glucose: 104 mg/dL
- Total cholesterol: 198 mg/dL

Your results place you in the “overweight/obese” BMI category, which is very common among employees in physically demanding trades. The provider noted that your weight has increased approximately 8 lbs compared to last year’s screening (234 lbs recorded 09/2023).

The nurse also documented your report of chronic gout and joint pain in your feet and ankles, and suggested that modest weight reduction could help ease stress on your lower extremity joints. As a reminder, the wellness program includes up to three free sessions with a health coach to discuss weight management, nutrition, and physical activity options that fit your schedule and medical needs.

Participation in follow-up coaching is voluntary and confidential. However, completing at least one coaching call will qualify you for the additional $10 per paycheck wellness incentive.

If you’d like to schedule a call, please contact our wellness partner, Heartland HealthWorks, at (800) 555‑8921 and reference your screening ID: GCM-2024-7712.

This information is for your personal use and will not be shared with your supervisor.

Best regards,
Amanda Lewis
HR & Benefits Coordinator
Green Country Mechanical Services, Inc.

artifacts.content:
Title: Home Weigh-In & BP Check – before infusion week
Date: 2025-02-09 06:45 AM–07:00 AM
Location: Bathroom scale / kitchen table
Attendees: Michael Harris

Description:

Recurring Sunday reminder Michael set on his phone to track weight and blood pressure at home the week before his IV gout infusions. He and Dr. Bennett agreed it would be helpful to have a few home readings, since his numbers sometimes look higher when he’s rushing at the clinic.

Checklist he typed into the event notes:
- Step on scale first thing after using the bathroom, before breakfast.
- Record weight in notebook (goal to stay under 235 lbs through spring).
- Sit at kitchen table, rest 5 minutes, then take BP with home cuff.
- Text Karen the numbers if she’s at early church service.

Last Sunday’s note (entered in description):
“2/2/25 – weight 229.8, BP 128/80, feet a little stiff but manageable. Slept better, only woke up once with toe throbbing. Need to lay off the chips during the game today.”

Michael uses these readings when the infusion nurse asks about changes in weight or swelling. He’s worried that if his weight creeps back up into the 240s again, it’ll put more strain on his ankles and make it harder to keep working full days on the truck.

metadata.title: Home Weigh-In & BP Check – before infusion week

artifacts.content:
From: Michael Harris <mharris4821@gmail.com>
To: Laura Bennett, MD <lbennett@tulsarheumclinic.com>
Date: 2025-01-15 07:42
Subject: Question about weight change and Krystexxa schedule

Hi Dr. Bennett,

I was looking over the paperwork your nurse gave me for the new IV gout medicine and noticed it still lists my weight from last summer’s visit at 224 lbs. When I was at the VA clinic on January 10th, they said I was up to about 242 pounds with my work boots off, which surprised me a little. Part of it is the holidays, I’m sure, but I’m also wondering if the prednisone 5 mg daily that Dr. Ortiz has me on is making me hold onto extra fluid.

At that VA appointment they also checked my uric acid and it was still 9.2 mg/dL, even with the prednisone and the on-and-off colchicine. He’s worried about how this is affecting my ability to keep working full time in the field for Green Country Mechanical Services, and that’s why he was so on board with starting the IV treatment.

Before we finalize the Krystexxa order (8 mg IV every 2 weeks with the standard premeds, if I understood correctly), do you need an updated weight from your office, or is the VA number fine? I can swing by your clinic early one morning this week to hop on your scale if that’s better for dosing.

Thanks,

Michael Harris
Tulsa, OK

metadata.subject: Question about weight change and Krystexxa schedule

Date: 2025-02-03 08:15–09:00
Location: Tulsa Rheumatology Clinic – 3rd Floor, Suite 310
Attendees: Michael Harris; Dr. Laura Bennett

Description:

Follow-up visit with Dr. Bennett to review first month of IV gout infusions and discuss side effects.

To bring:
- List of service calls missed due to ankle/toe pain
- Log of home blood pressures
- Log of home weights (started 1/5/25)

Weight notes for discussion (NOT official clinic weight):
- 1/05 – 236 lbs (after dinner, wearing jeans)
- 1/12 – 233 lbs (morning, in pajamas)
- 1/19 – 231 lbs (morning, in pajamas – after cutting back on barbecue and beer)

Ask Dr. Bennett:

3. At the VA clinic on 1/10/25 they said my uric acid was still 9.2 mg/dL even though Dr. Samuel Ortiz has me on prednisone 5 mg daily and intermittent colchicine – is that why he and you both want the Krystexxa 8 mg IV every 2 weeks?

Reminder: leave house by 7:30 a.m. to allow extra time for parking and walking slowly from the lot if feet are sore.

metadata.title: Rheumatology follow-up – bring home scale log

artifacts.content:
From: Karen Harris <kharris.home@gmail.com>
To: Michael Harris <mharris4821@gmail.com>
Date: 2026-03-08 21:03
Subject: Budget talk before your next infusion day

Mike,

I was going over the TTCU Federal Credit Union statements again tonight, and it still makes my stomach twist seeing how close we came to losing the house when we fell two months behind on the mortgage this January–February. I know missing about six weeks of work during that bad gout flare wasn’t your fault, but it really showed how tight things are when your paychecks drop.

Since you’re trying so hard to avoid any more unpaid time off, can you please double-check with the infusion center how long they really need you there? If those KRYSTEXXA infusions in Tulsa are going to run longer than two hours or they want you to stay for a bunch of observation time afterward, you’ve got to know in advance so you can ask Rick Dawson to put you on shorter service calls that day and not risk overtime cuts or another warning.

Also, you mentioned your work physical is coming up and they always write down your weight. Last year you said their scale had you at 246 lbs with your tool belt on, which freaked you out until you weighed 8–10 pounds less at home in just your jeans and t‑shirt. Maybe this time, step on the scale without the heavy boots so it doesn’t look like you gained a bunch.

Love you,
Karen

metadata.subject: Budget talk before your next infusion day

artifacts.content:
Title: Annual work physical – Green Country Mechanical
Date: 2025-06-18 07:30–08:30
Location: Occupational Health Clinic – 12th & Yale
Attendees: Michael Harris

Description:

Company-required annual physical for field technicians.

Checklist:
- Fasting labs (no breakfast beforehand)
- Blood pressure check
- Vision/hearing screening
- Weight and BMI (they usually record what the scale says with boots on)
- Basic mobility test (ladder step, squat, grip strength)

Personal notes:
- Ask nurse if I can step on the scale without steel-toe boots and tool belt this time. Last year I was around 240 lbs on their scale, but at home that same week I was closer to 232–233 in just regular clothes.
- Mention ongoing gout issues in toes, ankles, and elbows, and that I’ve started IV treatment every two weeks.
- Clarify with clinic whether they send any info about my weight or work restrictions directly to Rick Dawson or just to HR.

Reminder to self: Don’t stress if their number is a few pounds higher than what I see on our bathroom scale. The important thing is that I’m slowly trending down from where I was a couple of years ago when I crept up into the mid‑240s.

Plan: After appointment, grab a light breakfast and then head to first service call on the west side by 9:00 a.m. if joints are cooperating.

metadata.title: Annual work physical – Green Country Mechanical

From: Michael Harris <maharris62@gmail.com>

Hi Jenna,

You left a voicemail yesterday about finishing the online forms before my follow-up with Dr. Bennett on March 18, so I wanted to confirm a couple of details you asked for and clarify my medication issues.

Demographics first: I’m Michael Anthony Harris, DOB 11/14/1963, male, 5'10" (178 cm), around 204 pounds as of last week. I live at 4821 S Hudson Avenue here in Tulsa. I’ve been working as a senior HVAC technician for Green Country Mechanical Services for the last 12 years, mostly on commercial rooftop units.

On the medical history section, there was a question about drug allergies and intolerances. Please make sure colchicine is clearly listed on my avoidance list. Even low doses made me violently sick to my stomach during a flare in February 2022—I spent two days running back and forth to the bathroom at our Hudson Avenue house, missed a full 10-hour shift at Green Country Mechanical, and my supervisor, Mark Delaney, had to reassign a $4,800 rooftop HVAC job at Woodland Hills Mall because I couldn’t be off the toilet long enough to drive there.

I’m currently on low-dose prednisone daily and coming in every other Tuesday morning for the IV gout treatment. I usually schedule the infusions early so I can still work a half day afterward.

If anything is missing from the chart, you can reach me at this email or on my cell at 918-555-0198.

Thanks,

Michael Harris

metadata.subject: Pre-visit paperwork for 3/18 rheumatology appointment

artifacts.content:
From: Michael Harris <mharris62@gmail.com>
To: New Patient Coordinator <newpatients@oklahomarheum.com>
Date: 2025-01-07 08:14 AM
Subject: Question about intake forms (height/weight section)

Hi there,

I started filling out the online intake packet for my upcoming visit with Dr. Bennett and got hung up on the height/weight page. My home scale is ancient and I don’t totally trust it, but it says I’m right around 212 pounds. As for height, I’ve always told people I’m "about five-nine" ever since high school physicals, though the nurse at my primary care’s office a couple years ago said I’d "shrunk a bit" and was closer to five-eight and a half. I’m not sure which you prefer for your records.

Do you need an exact measurement before my first visit, or will it be rechecked in the office anyway? I don’t want to put something that’s going to be off by an inch or two and mess up any of your dosing calculations.

Also, just so it’s in my chart somewhere: I’ve been seeing Dr. Alan McKenzie, a psychiatrist at Laureate Psychiatric Clinic, since October 2024 for depression tied to my chronic pain and my dad Robert’s death back in 2018. We recently bumped my sertraline from 50 mg to 100 mg daily. If I seem overly anxious about the KRYSTEXXA infusions or ask a lot of questions about side effects, that’s partly why. Please don’t bring this up with my wife unless I’m in the room.

Thanks for your help sorting this out.

Best,
Michael Harris

metadata.subject: Question about intake forms (height/weight section)

artifacts.content:
From: Karen Harris <karenharris_ok@yahoo.com>
To: Michael Harris <mharris62@gmail.com>
Date: 2024-12-02 09:21 PM
Subject: Liam’s growth chart vs. yours :)

Mike,

I was putting away Liam’s school pictures and found that little growth chart the pediatrician gave Emily. She texted me his stats from his last checkup – 44" tall and 43 pounds. Can you believe he’s already half my height? No wonder my back hurts when I carry him.

It reminded me of when we used to mark the kids’ heights on the pantry door at the old house. You always joked that Daniel was going to pass you and end up the tallest in the family. For the record, I still remember the nurse at Dr. Lawson’s office measuring you at just under five-eight without shoes that one year you had your physical for the new HVAC job. You grumbled all the way home that they must have used a crooked ruler because you’d "always been five-nine and a half." 

Anyway, when you go in for that infusion consult, let them measure you again properly. Between your back and those gouty toes, I’m sure you slouch more than you realize. I don’t want them underestimating you and giving you the wrong dose of anything because the chart says you’re shorter than you really are.

Don’t stay up too late reading those HVAC forums. Remember your 5:30 alarm.

Love you,
Karen

metadata.subject: Liam’s growth chart vs. yours :)

artifacts.content:
Title: Annual Physical – Dr. Lawson (height/weight check)
Date: 2023-06-19 08:00 AM–09:00 AM
Location: Midtown Family Medicine, 61st & Yale
Attendees: Michael Harris, Karen Harris (possible)

Notes:

Bring insurance card, medication list, and most recent labs from rheumatology.

Last year at this visit, nurse commented that I’d "lost an inch" compared to my chart from my 40s. She measured me at around 5'8" when I was standing barefoot against the wall, which surprised me because I’ve always told people I was closer to 5'9". Might be the years of crawling around attics and on roofs catching up with my spine.

Remember to mention:
- Frequency of nighttime foot/ankle pain waking me up.
- How often I have to limit ladder climbing at work.
- That I’m still doing the early-morning stretching routine.

Also need to update him that I started seeing Dr. Alan McKenzie at Laureate Psychiatric Clinic in October 2024 for depression tied to chronic pain and my dad Robert’s death in 2018. Sertraline dose was increased from 50 mg to 100 mg daily, and it’s helping some with the constant worrying. If he brings up weight or height changes, I want him to know the mental side of all this too.

Fasting not required, but try not to eat a big greasy breakfast beforehand in case they check cholesterol.

Plan: Go straight from appointment to first service call near 71st & Memorial.

metadata.title: Annual Physical – Dr. Lawson (height/weight check)

artifacts.content:
From: Daniel Harris <dan.harris.tulsa@gmail.com>
To: Michael Harris <mharris62@gmail.com>
Date: 2024-08-10 03:47 PM
Subject: Work boots – need your exact size & height

Hey Dad,

I’m ordering those composite-toe work boots I told you about. The site is running a sale this weekend only. They’ve got a section where you can plug in your height and weight so they can recommend the right insole and arch support.

I know you’ve always said you were "about five-nine" back when you were climbing roofs all day, but didn’t the urgent care nurse last year say you were closer to five-eight in your socks? I remember you joking that you were shrinking like Grandpa Robert. Either way, I need something to put in the form so they don’t send you the wrong support level.

If you don’t remember exactly, I’ll just put 5'9" and 210 lbs and call it good. The main thing is getting something that doesn’t make your ankles scream when you’re on concrete all day. They also ask if you use orthotics or lifts, so let me know that too.

Text me your current shoe size and whether you want the medium or high ankle. I’d go high for more support, but I know you hate feeling "locked in" when your gout is acting up.

Love,
Dan

Check in early for KRYSTEXXA pre-infusion screening. They said they’ll:

artifacts.content:
From: intake@greenvalleyrheum.com
To: Michael Harris <maharris62@gmail.com>
Subject: Clarification Needed – New Patient Paperwork
Date: 2024-01-03 8:14 AM

Dear Mr. Harris,

Thank you for submitting your new patient forms ahead of your upcoming appointment with Dr. Laura Bennett on January 9.

Our staff noticed a small discrepancy in the demographic section and wanted to confirm a detail before your visit. On the online intake form, the “Height” field appears to have been left blank, but in the attached photo of your old orthopedic clinic card you sent, your height is listed as **5'8"**. In a later message in the portal, you mentioned your “driver’s license still says 5'11" from when I was younger,” which adds to our uncertainty.

For medication dosing and infusion planning, we need your current, most accurate height. If you have been measured recently at your primary care physician’s office and the value has changed from 5'8" due to age-related height loss or back issues, please reply with the updated measurement in feet and inches. If 5'8" is still correct, simply reply "confirm 5'8"" so we can update your chart.

We understand this may seem minor, but accurate height and weight help us calculate appropriate dosing for IV therapies, especially given your history of steroid use and chronic gout. Please respond at your earliest convenience so we can finalize your chart before your appointment.

Sincerely,

Rachel Moore, RN
Green Valley Rheumatology Clinic
Tulsa, OK

metadata.subject: Clarification Needed – New Patient Paperwork

artifacts.content:
From: okdmv-notify@ok.gov
To: Michael A. Harris <maharris62@gmail.com>
Subject: Driver License Renewal – Verify Physical Details
Date: 2023-09-18 6:42 PM

Dear MICHAEL ANTHONY HARRIS,

Our records indicate that your Oklahoma Class D driver license is due for renewal on 2024-01-02. As part of the online renewal process, please verify that your physical description is still accurate.

According to our database, your current information is:

Height: **5-11**
Weight: 212 lb
Eye Color: BLU
Hair Color: GRY

If your height has changed since your last in-person renewal (for example, due to age-related changes, back or joint issues), you may update this value during the online renewal process or at a Service Oklahoma location. For most small changes, documentation is not required, but if there has been a significant discrepancy (e.g., more than 3 inches from the last recorded height of 5-11), you may be asked to provide verification at the time of renewal.

Please note that providing accurate information is important for identification and emergency medical purposes. However, minor variations (such as daily weight fluctuation) do not need to be reported.

To begin your renewal, visit:
https://renew.oklahoma.gov

For questions, contact Service Oklahoma Customer Support at (405) 555-0198.

Thank you,
Service Oklahoma – Driver License Services

metadata.subject: Driver License Renewal – Verify Physical Details

Date: 2026-02-09 08:14 AM
Subject: KRYSTEXXA initiation – diagnosis coding and scheduling

Hi Mr. Harris,

Following up on your visit last week, I’ve started the KRYSTEXXA prior authorization paperwork with your insurance. For the “krystexxa order details > ICD-10” line, I initially entered M1A.09X1 (chronic gout with tophus, multiple sites) based on your chart summary, but your ultrasound report emphasizes more focal involvement of the right ankle and midfoot. I may revise the diagnosis code to something more specific once I review the full radiology images and your prior rheumatology notes.

To avoid any delays, the infusion center may see a few different gout-related ICD-10 codes in the early documentation (for example, M10.071 or M1A.0710 were used by urgent care during your Labor Day flare). Don’t worry if you see those on paperwork; they’re all within the same clinical picture of chronic tophaceous gout and won’t change the actual treatment you receive.

You also mentioned that you’re listed as allergic to penicillin after a childhood reaction with rash and hives back in June 1969, when Dr. Harold Pruitt treated you for strep throat and your mom rushed you to the St. Francis ER. I’ve added that to our EHR allergy list so the infusion nurses are aware.

Once insurance responds, the infusion center will call you to finalize dates. I noted your preference to avoid Monday and Wednesday late afternoons because of your wife’s oncology appointments.

Best,

Laura Bennett, MD
Tulsa Rheumatology Clinic

artifacts.content:
From: Michael Harris <mharris62@gmail.com>
To: Rheumatology Scheduling <appointments@tulsarheumclinic.com>
Date: 2025-01-08 07:42
Subject: Question about ICD-10 code on Krystexxa order

Hi there,

I was looking over the after-visit summary from yesterday and noticed the diagnosis section lists “chronic gout due to renal impairment, right ankle and foot” with ICD-10 code **M1A.3711** next to the Krystexxa infusion orders. I just wanted to double-check that this is the correct code you intend to use going forward so there aren’t any insurance hiccups.

When I talked with the billing person at the infusion center, she mentioned that sometimes chronic gout gets coded under different numbers like M10.071 or M1A.09X1 depending on whether it’s considered idiopathic or related to kidneys. Since the ER doctor at St. John Medical Center told me last May that my kidneys were “borderline,” I figured it’s better to ask now than fight with the insurance company later.

Also, please remember on the renewal that I had to stop allopurinol and febuxostat last year after I broke out in hives and had trouble breathing during an ER visit at St. John on May 22, 2024. That’s when they also suggested I think about cutting back overtime shifts at Green Country Mechanical Services, which I’ve been trying (not always successfully) to do.

If M1A.3711 isn’t the right ICD-10 for the Krystexxa orders, can you let me know which one you’ll be using so I can give the correct info to my insurance rep?

Thanks,

Michael A. Harris
Tulsa, OK

metadata.title: Krystexxa Infusion + Insurance Coding Check

artifacts.content:
From: BCBS of Oklahoma – Utilization Review <noreply@bcbsok.com>
To: Michael Harris <mharris62@gmail.com>
Date: 2025-01-11 11:03
Subject: Prior Authorization Decision – Krystexxa Therapy

Dear Michael A. Harris,

We have completed review of your provider’s request for continued pegloticase (Krystexxa) therapy.

Service requested: KRYSTEXXA 8 mg IV, every 2 weeks, outpatient infusion

Primary diagnosis submitted: Chronic idiopathic gout with tophus, right ankle and foot
ICD-10 code submitted by provider: **M1A.0712**

Our medical policy allows for coverage of Krystexxa when the following criteria are met:
• Documented chronic gout with failure or intolerance to xanthine oxidase inhibitors (e.g., allopurinol, febuxostat)
• Recurrent flares and/or presence of tophi
• Treatment ordered and monitored by a rheumatologist

Your provider documented that you discontinued both allopurinol and febuxostat after developing hives and shortness of breath, requiring evaluation in the emergency department at St. John Medical Center on 05/22/2024. The ER note also referenced “borderline renal function” and recommended considering reduction of overtime work at Green Country Mechanical Services.

Decision: APPROVED

Coverage dates: 01/15/2025 – 07/15/2025
Authorization number: 24-OK-173992

Please note: This approval is based on ICD-10 code M1A.0712 as submitted. Any changes in diagnosis coding (for example, to M1A.09X1 or other gout-related codes) may require a new review.

This notice is not a guarantee of payment. Benefits are subject to member eligibility at the time of service.

Sincerely,
Utilization Review Department
Blue Cross Blue Shield of Oklahoma

metadata.subject: Prior Authorization Decision – Krystexxa Therapy

artifacts.content:
From: Laura Bennett, MD <lbennett@tulsarheumclinic.com>
To: Michael Harris <maharris62@gmail.com>
Date: 2025-11-13 3:42 PM
Subject: KRYSTEXXA infusion schedule and work documentation

Hi Mr. Harris,

It was good seeing you yesterday. I’m glad to hear your ankle has been a little more manageable with the current regimen.

To summarize our plan:

• Your next KRYSTEXXA infusion is scheduled for **Wednesday, November 26, 2025 at 8:00 a.m.** at the Allergy, Asthma & Immunology Center, infusion suite, 2nd floor.
• Please arrive 20–30 minutes early so nursing staff can start your premedications.
• Typical chair time has been running about 2 hours, but if there are any delays we will make sure to document exact start and stop times on your visit summary.

You mentioned that because of the workers’ comp claim you filed after your fall from the rooftop unit on June 27, 2023 at the Reasor’s on E 41st Street, your company’s HR manager, Linda Perez, and the workers’ comp carrier are strict about any time you miss for medical visits. We will continue to print your infusion records so you can show that you are not abusing medical leave.

If your employer needs a letter confirming that your last infusion prior to this upcoming one was earlier in the fall (in September) and that these are medically necessary for uncontrolled chronic gout, I’m happy to provide that.

Please call the clinic if you experience any new symptoms or need to adjust the appointment time.

Best,
Laura Bennett, MD
Tulsa Rheumatology Associates

artifacts.content:
Title: KRYSTEXXA Infusion – bring work paperwork
Date: 2025-10-29 07:45–10:15
Location: Allergy, Asthma & Immunology Center – Infusion Suite
Attendees: Michael Harris

Description:

Remember: this is the **second-to-last infusion of the year**. Last one was earlier this month, and Dr. Bennett said we’d reassess after a few more rounds.

• Check in by 7:45 a.m.; actual infusion supposed to start at 8:00 a.m.
• Ask the nurse to write down **exact start and stop times** on the paperwork – HR and the workers’ comp people keep grilling me about how long I’m gone.
• Bring the folder with copies of the incident report from the fall off the rooftop unit at the Reasor’s on E 41st (June 27, 2023). Ever since that workers’ comp claim, Linda in HR and the comp carrier have been nitpicky about every hour I’m out for medical stuff.
• Take prednisone and antihistamine at home by 6:45 a.m.
• Park in the back lot so it’s easier on my ankle.

Note to self: tell them that the last infusion earlier in October went okay except for the headache that night. Ask if that could be from the premeds. Also double-check with the infusion nurse about how many weeks we’ll keep this schedule before they consider spacing them out.

Try to be out of there by 10:15 a.m. so I can still make the afternoon service call on the strip mall RTU over on South Mingo.

metadata.title: KRYSTEXXA Infusion – bring work paperwork
metadata.location: Allergy, Asthma & Immunology Center – Infusion Suite

artifacts.content:
From: Linda Perez <lperez@greencountrymech.com>
To: Michael Harris <mharris@greencountrymech.com>
Date: 2025-09-03 9:07 AM
Subject: Documentation needed for infusion appointments

Mike,

Following up on our conversation yesterday about your gout infusions.

Per our workers’ comp carrier’s guidelines, we need clear documentation for **each** KRYSTEXXA appointment showing:

1. Date of service
2. Check-in time
3. Infusion start and stop times
4. Discharge time (if different)

I have records for your visits on 07/10/2025 and 08/07/2025, but the paperwork from your most recent infusion in late August doesn’t list the stop time clearly. Next time you’re at the Allergy, Asthma & Immunology Center, please ask the infusion nurse to note both the start and end times on the visit summary.

Because of the workers’ comp claim you filed after the fall from the rooftop unit at the Reasor’s on E 41st Street on June 27, 2023, the carrier audits any time off that could be related to that injury. I’m not saying you’re abusing leave, but I do need the documentation to protect both you and the company if they question your hours.

Going forward, if you can keep these appointments as close as possible to first thing in the morning (like you did with the 09/02/2025 infusion), that will minimize impact on scheduling and help me justify any partial-day absences.

Thanks,
Linda

Linda Perez
HR Manager
Green Country Mechanical Services, Inc.

Description:

Agenda for visit with Dr. Bennett:

• Bring printed records for the KRYSTEXXA infusions from **September, October, and November 2025**. I need her to write a short note summarizing how I tolerated those and confirming they’re medically necessary for chronic tophaceous gout.
• Ask her to list the most recent infusion date in the letter for HR (the one right after Thanksgiving) and include the usual start/stop times. That should satisfy Linda and the workers’ comp folks.
• Mention that the infusion I had just before Christmas got rescheduled because of that ice storm, so the dates on the clinic’s original reminder messages are wrong. Make sure they correct that in their system.
• Double-check if we’re staying on the every-two-week schedule into the spring or if she wants to stretch it out to monthly.

Also need to remind her that ever since the fall from the rooftop unit at the Reasor’s on E 41st back on 06/27/2023, HR and the carrier have been picky about my time away from job sites. I need her to spell out that I have to stay at the infusion center the whole time they’re monitoring me, not just for the drip itself.

Bring list of questions from Karen about long-term side effects.

Date: 2025-11-19 07:12 AM
Subject: Confirmation – Rescheduled Gout Infusion Appointment

Dear Mr. Harris,

This message confirms that your IV infusion for uncontrolled chronic tophaceous gout has been successfully **rescheduled to Wednesday, November 26, 2025 at 7:30 a.m.** at our South Sheridan infusion suite.

Please arrive 20–30 minutes early to allow time for check‑in and premedication. As a reminder, your current plan is **infusions every 2 weeks**, and we have noted your request to **avoid the second Wednesday of each month** due to your standing psychiatric follow‑up with **Dr. Kaplan at Laureate Psychiatric Clinic** for management of major depressive disorder.

Your recent lab work from earlier this month showed continued improvement in serum uric acid levels. Dr. Bennett would like you to:

• Continue your current low‑dose prednisone unless instructed otherwise.
• Maintain adequate hydration the day before and day of infusion.
• Report any infusion reactions or new joint tophi.

Your **previous infusion was documented on Wednesday, November 12, 2025**, and your next tentative visit after 11/26/2025 will fall two weeks later, pending any scheduling changes you request related to work or other appointments.

If you need to adjust this appointment because of your HVAC on‑call schedule or other obligations, please call our office at least 24 hours in advance so we can keep you on your every‑other‑week cycle.

Sincerely,

Julie Martinez, RN
Infusion Coordinator
Oklahoma Rheumatology & Arthritis Care
(918) 555‑0194

Description:

Early‑morning IV infusion for chronic tophaceous gout.

Reminders:
- Keep this as part of the **every‑other‑Wednesday** pattern.

Recent timeline (for my own reference):

Day‑of plan:
- Leave house by 6:15 a.m. to make 7:00 a.m. check‑in.

- Try to be out of there by 10:00 a.m. so I can still grab a couple of lighter HVAC calls in the afternoon.

- If I feel okay, stop by Mom’s in Sand Springs on the way home from the last call.

metadata.title: Rheumatology – Infusion + Labs (avoid 2nd Wednesday)

artifacts.content:
From: laura.bennett.md@okrheumcare.com
To: maharris62@gmail.com
Date: 2025-12-03 05:56 PM
Subject: Adjustments to Infusion Timing Over Holidays

Hi Michael,

I reviewed your chart and the nurse’s notes from your most recent visit on **Wednesday, December 3, 2025**, when you received your latest gout infusion. I’m glad to hear the ankle swelling has started to come down and that you were able to get through last weekend’s HVAC calls without needing crutches.

Because of the upcoming holidays and your work schedule, I’d like to keep your treatment close to the **every‑2‑weeks** rhythm without overloading you. The infusion you just had on **12/03/2025** will serve as your early‑December dose. Instead of bringing you back exactly two weeks later, the infusion desk will look at dates the week of **December 15–19, 2025**, and try to pair that with a lighter day for you at Green Country Mechanical.

Please let the team know which days you’re on emergency call so they can avoid stacking a long infusion with a late‑night furnace outage. If your symptoms flare significantly before your next scheduled infusion, contact us right away rather than waiting for your routine slot.

Also, continue limiting beer and high‑purine foods during the holiday gatherings. I know that’s not fun, but it will help keep you out of urgent care.

Take care,

Laura Bennett, MD
Rheumatology
Oklahoma Rheumatology & Arthritis Care
